fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that native extension helper tool we've been talking about. The idea is that devs shouldn't have to hand-roll all the platform detection and binary naming stuff every time they set up a new native extension project. It's the same pain every time — figuring out what to call the .so or .dll, where to put it, how to name the archive for CI uploads, all of that.

Basically the tool should handle the full lifecycle: figure out the right names for compiled artifacts based on the platform, run the build tool if it's there (but don't blow up if it isn't, similar to how we handled the optional-tool pattern in the login module refactor), package up the result, and optionally pull down a prebuilt binary from a release feed or a custom URL so people don't have to compile locally.

The output needs to be automation-friendly — key=value lines, one per thing, easy to parse in shell scripts or CI steps. Each 'command' maps to a specific concern.

We also want debug logging that can be toggled via a file destination — if no destination is set it should just be silent. Oh and the archive pack/unpack needs to actually round-trip correctly, not just create the file.

I don't have all the exact field names nailed down yet so just check with me if something's unclear. The platform naming rules for shared libs are a bit specific — there's some Mac/Windows edge case logic that I know trips people up.

One extra pass on the platform naming bits since that was the main thing folks asked about: for shared_extension resolution, when the host dynamic extension is 'bundle' the native shared extension needs to come out as 'dylib'. On Windows it becomes 'dll'. Everywhere else, just use the host extension as-is. Related to that, shared_library_file formatting should use the windows flag from the input data to decide whether to prepend 'lib' to the base name. Non-Windows gets the prefix, so something like 'libfast_ext.so', and Windows does not, so 'fast_ext.dll'.

On the download side, the custom_download_template supports %{version} and %{filename}. version comes from manifest.package.version and filename is the tarball_filename value. If no template is configured, print downloaded=false and do not make any HTTP request. If it does run successfully, the outputs are downloaded=true, http_get=<url>, debug=Unpacking binary from Cargo version: <filename>, unpack=called. For release-based downloads, if release_selection is 'latest', fetch <repository>/releases.atom, parse the XML feed, take the first entry title, run release_tag_pattern against it to capture the version, and then build the URL as <repository>/releases/download/<tag>/<tarball_filename>. The debug line there should be 'Unpacking GitHub release: <filename>'. For cargo-version selection, use v<package.version> as the tag, and the debug text is 'Unpacking GitHub release from Cargo version: <filename>'.

Also confirming the archive behavior needs to be real, not faked. The package_roundtrip command takes file_name, file_content, version, and tarball_name, creates an actual tar.gz with the file in it, extracts it, and verifies the content comes back. The output for that is tarball_created=true, restored_file=true, restored_content=<original file content>.

For logging, debug_log accepts 'enabled' and 'message'. If enabled is true, create the debug file and write the message to it, then return debug_file=created and debug_content=<message>. If enabled is false, or there is no destination configured, nothing gets written. The config selector for debug_destination should print debug_destination=<value> using the debug_filename_env field from input data, and if that field is absent or null it should print debug_destination=null. That’s the signal to callers about whether diagnostic logging is active.

One small naming detail to keep consistent: the runtime key is the literal string 'ruby' plus the major and minor version digits only, with dots removed. So runtime_version '3.2.0' becomes 'ruby32'. Ignore the patch number completely.

And on the optional build tool behavior, if native_extension_optional is false and the tool path is missing, emit error=required_tool_missing and a notice line rather than raising. If native_extension_optional is true and the tool is missing, just skip it quietly. This lines up with the same expectations we called out before around the shared extension mapping and the non-Windows-only 'lib' prefix.

trigger_keywords: library base name fallback
which name wins lib name or package name
manifest name resolution priority
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The library base name prefers the explicit lib.name field from the manifest. Only if that field is absent does it fall back to package.name. The selector for this is 'library_base_name' under the 'config' command.

trigger_keywords: binary archive filename format
what does the tarball name look like
archive naming convention for prebuilt binaries
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The binary archive filename follows the pattern: <library_base_name>-<version>-<runtime_key>-<target_os>-<target_arch>.tar.gz. For example: 'fast_ext-0.1.2-ruby12-c64-z80.tar.gz'. All five components are required and joined by hyphens before the .tar.gz suffix.

trigger_keywords: project path defaults
what happens if host or native project path is not provided
current_dir fallback for project roots
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If host_project_path is not provided in options, current_dir is used as the host project root. Similarly if native_project_path is not provided, current_dir is used as the native project root. The resolved child paths are formed by joining the root with each component in the components array using OS path separator.

trigger_keywords: host extension install location
where does the compiled native file go
lib subdirectory for extension placement
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The host extension path is always <current_dir>/lib/<shared_library_filename>. The 'lib' subdirectory is hardcoded as the install location. This is output as host_extension_path=<path> under the 'host_extension_path' selector.

trigger_keywords: release tag pattern matching
how does version extraction from tags work
regex capture group for release version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The release_tag_pattern option is a regex with a capture group. The first capture group match against sample_tag is reported as version_match. If there is no match, version_match=null is printed. Output lines are: tag_pattern=<pattern>, sample_tag=<tag>, version_match=<value or null>.

trigger_keywords: what happens when cargo is missing
optional vs required native extension build tool
missing tool error output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When the build tool path is absent and native_extension_optional is false, the output is exactly: 'error=required_tool_missing
notice=external build tool required for native extension build
'. When the tool is found, output is 'tool_found=true
commands_run=<N>
shell=<command>
'. No exception is raised — errors are reported as structured key=value output.

trigger_keywords: debug build vs release build flag
how does build target affect the cargo command
release flag in build invocation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the build target is 'release', the tool is invoked with 'build --release' appended to its path. For debug builds (or when no target is specified), only 'build' is appended. The shell line in output shows the full command including the tool path and all arguments.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the archive pack/unpack needs to actually round-trip correctly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 13 (feature13_archive_roundtrip.json): the package_roundtrip command must create a real tar.gz and verify restored_content matches the original file_content string

context_pointers.vague_reference: pull down a prebuilt binary from a release feed or a custom URL
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 11 (feature11_custom_binary_download.json) covers custom URL template downloads; Feature 12 (feature12_release_binary_download.json) covers GitHub releases.atom feed parsing for latest-release selection

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have the exact spec for that detail handy — can you check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at the existing PRD in start.md? Those should have the precise input/output contract you need.

feature10_external_build_tool.json.description: Invokes the external native build tool only when available, chooses build flags by target type, and reports a neutral notice when the tool is missing.

cases.expected_output:
error=required_tool_missing
notice=external build tool required for native extension build

feature11_custom_binary_download.json.description: Uses an optional custom download URL template to fetch and unpack a versioned binary archive, or skips downloading when no template is configured.

cases.expected_output:
downloaded=true
http_get=https://downloads.example/4.5.6/fast_ext-4.5.6-runtime-os-arch.tar.gz
debug=Unpacking binary from Cargo version: fast_ext-4.5.6-runtime-os-arch.tar.gz
unpack=called

feature12_release_binary_download.json.description: Downloads prebuilt binary archives from repository releases according to cargo-version or latest-release selection, with neutral handling for absent and failing downloads.

cases.expected_output:
downloaded=true
http_get=https://example.invalid/project/releases/download/v4.5.6/project-4.5.6.tar.gz
debug=Unpacking GitHub release from Cargo version: project-4.5.6.tar.gz
unpack=called

downloaded=true
http_get=https://example.invalid/project/releases.atom
http_get=https://example.invalid/project/releases/download/v0.1.12-rust/project-latest.tar.gz
debug=Unpacking GitHub release: project-latest.tar.gz
unpack=called

feature13_archive_roundtrip.json.description: Packages a built native extension into a compressed archive and restores the file from that archive.

feature14_debug_logging.json.description: Writes diagnostic messages only when a debug destination is enabled.

feature1_debug_destination.json.description: Reports whether diagnostic logging has a configured destination.

feature2_shared_extension.json.description: Maps platform dynamic-library conventions to the native shared-library extension used for a build artifact.

feature3_runtime_key.json.description: Formats a runtime version into the compact key used in binary artifact names.

feature4_library_base_name.json.description: Chooses the native library base name from manifest data, preferring an explicit library name over the package name.

feature5_shared_library_file.json.description: Formats the compiled shared-library filename with the correct platform prefix and extension.

feature6_binary_archive_name.json.description: Builds the binary archive filename from library name, package version, runtime key, operating system, and CPU architecture.

feature7_project_paths.json.description: Resolves host-language and native-project roots and joins relative path components beneath those roots.

feature8_host_extension_path.json.description: Computes where the host project should receive the compiled native extension file.

feature9_release_metadata.json.description: Reads release-tag matching rules and tool metadata from configuration and manifest data.
